result:
Two main strategies.

Chaining: each bucket holds a linked list (or small tree). Colliding keys get appended. Lookup walks list, comparing keys. Simple, degrades gracefully, wastes pointer memory.

Open addressing: all entries live in array itself. On collision, probe for next free slot.
- Linear probing: check next slot, then next. Cache-friendly but clusters.
- Quadratic probing: step by 1, 4, 9... breaks up clusters.
- Double hashing: second hash decides step size. Best distribution, worse cache.

Deletion in open addressing needs tombstones so probe chains stay intact.

Load factor matters. Chaining tolerates >1. Open addressing needs resize around 0.7 or performance collapses.

Real-world: Java HashMap chains, swaps to red-black tree past 8 entries per bucket. Python dict, Go map, Rust HashBrown use open addressing variants. Robin Hood hashing (steal from rich probes) keeps worst-case lookup tight.
